caution.intro: Approach calmly and without accusation. Establish rapport, then account. Caution correctly before any question aimed at an offence.

cards.body: "You are not obliged to say anything unless you wish to do so, but whatever you do say will be taken down in writing and may be given in evidence." Ask: "Do you understand that?" — record the reply.
Once you have reasonable grounds to suspect a person of an offence and intend to ask questions about it. Voluntary res gestae utterances before that need no caution but must be recorded verbatim.

Open non-confrontationally: "I'm making enquiries about X, I'd like to hear your account." Let them talk freely first (free narrative) before challenge. Do not reveal all evidence at once. Note demeanour, spontaneous comments, first reaction.

Movements (times/places), who was present, what they saw/did, means/opportunity, anything handled, calls/messages, any alibi. Open questions ("tell me…", "what happened…") not leading ones. Clarify, don't suggest.

cards.h: Statutory declaration on a witness statement
cards.body: "I hereby declare that this statement is true to the best of my knowledge and belief and that I make it knowing that if it is tendered in evidence I will be liable to prosecution if I state in it anything which I know to be false or do not believe to be true."

Suspect writes/dictates freely after caution. Record: caution given and understood, statement made voluntarily, no threat/inducement/oppression, read back, invited to correct, signed. This is the pre-caution voluntary statement, distinct from a detention interview.
Time, date, place; grounds of suspicion; caution administered and reply; questions and answers verbatim; any admission in the person's own words in quotes; that notes were made contemporaneously.

cases.p: Voluntariness — confession must not be procured by threat, inducement or oppression.
Reason for arrest must be given in ordinary language at the time.
Right of access to a solicitor before questioning; admissions taken before consultation excluded.
Guidance on cautioning and the taking of voluntary statements — breach goes to admissibility/weight.
Caution and fairness in questioning; voluntariness of admissions.

terms: The guilty act — the physical element of an offence. Use when analysing proofs.
The guilty mind — intent, knowledge, recklessness, dishonesty.
Bad faith. Opposite of bona fides (good faith).
Good faith — e.g. a claim of right made bona fide (theft defence).
On its face — enough evidence to answer a charge absent rebuttal.
The place in which — the scene of the incident.
Things done — spontaneous statements so closely connected to the event they are admissible as an exception to hearsay.
Method of operating — an offender's characteristic way of working.
From one side only — application without the other party present.
In private — court closed to the public.
Under judicial consideration — matter before the court.
Beyond the powers — act outside legal authority.
A trial within a trial — to decide admissibility of evidence in the jury's absence.
Unwilling to pursue — DPP declines to proceed.
Produce the body — challenge to lawfulness of detention (Art 40.4).
Hear the other side — fair procedures.
No one a judge in their own cause — bias rule (see Damache).
The body of the crime — that an offence actually occurred.
To one who consents no injury is done.
Having discharged the duty — no longer having authority to act.

items: Assume nothing · Believe nothing · Challenge/Corroborate everything. The investigator's mindset.
Means · Motive · Opportunity — the three pillars linking a suspect to an offence.
Amount of time · Distance · Visibility · Obstruction · Known before · Any reason to remember · Time lapse · Errors — quality of an ID.
Break every offence into its statutory elements before charging.
Report, Undress/observe, Note scene, — pathologist for suspicious deaths.
Who · What · Where · When · Why · (How) — cover in every account.
Preserve · Log · Access control · Note — first at scene.
Keep Local Officer informed — command & control information flow.
Size · Activity · Location · Unit · Time · Equipment — structured observation report.

cards.h: Possession of a knife — s.9(1) FO(OW)A 1990
cards.body: Has with them in a public place any knife or other article with a blade or sharply pointed, without lawful authority or reasonable excuse. Onus of excuse on the accused. Arrestable.

cards.h: Offensive weapon in public — s.9(4) / s.9(5)
cards.body: Possession of any article intended to cause injury / made or adapted for that purpose. Includes items innocent in themselves but carried with intent.

cards.h: Trespassing with a knife / weapon — s.10
cards.body: Enters a building or curtilage as a trespasser with an offensive weapon or knife.
Produces in a manner likely to intimidate an article capable of inflicting serious injury, while committing/ during a dispute.
s.23 FO(OW)A 1990: stop, search, detain person or vehicle on reasonable suspicion of a s.9/10/11 offence; seize the article. Note grounds carefully.
Work use, tool in transit, sporting use — test the account against the circumstances, time, place, packaging. Record their explanation verbatim.

searches.intro: Identify your power BEFORE you search. Record the grounds. Distinguish consent from statutory power — never blur them.

cards.body: Ask plainly: "Do you mind if I search you / the car? You don't have to consent." Record: consent freely given, person told they could refuse, nothing said or done to pressure. If they hand something over: "He produced it voluntarily from his pocket."

Be specific and articulable: "I formed the suspicion based on [furtive movement / smell of cannabis / known to me for X / bulge consistent with / area and time / behaviour on seeing me]." Vague hunches fail — tie it to what you observed.
Reasonable suspicion person in possession of a controlled drug — search person/vehicle; seize. State the grounds.
Arrest/search re scheduled offences and firearms/explosives — strict conditions, verify applicability.
Search of person detained; record everything found and continuity.

cards.h: Vehicle stop — s.109 RTA / checkpoint powers
cards.body: Lawful stop, then articulate any further search power. Don't conflate a traffic stop with a search power.
Note: power relied on, grounds, time/place, what found, what seized (exhibit ref), what said. A search good in fact but unrecorded is a search you may lose.

items: Preserve life; make safe; secondary-collision risk
Note final rest positions before anything moves — photograph
Debris field, skid/scuff/gouge marks, point of impact
Weather, light, road surface, visibility, sightlines
Speed limit, road markings, signals, obstructions
Vehicles: make/model/reg, damage mapped, VIN if needed
Identify all drivers; nominate driver; note admissions at scene verbatim
Sobriety: preliminary breath/impairment where lawful (s.4/s.5 RTA)
Independent witnesses — positions, what each actually saw
Dashcams, doorbell, CCTV — canvass and preserve immediately
Measurements & sketch — or forensic collision investigator
Phone data if distraction suspected (proper legal basis)
Photograph everything twice — overview and detail
Public place, driving, identity of driver
Manner of driving (careless s.52 / dangerous s.53) with facts
Map to the charge you intend

cards.h: Legal status (RTA 1961 as amended / RSA regs)
cards.body: E-scooters were regularised as 'powered personal transporters' under the Road Traffic and Roads Act 2023 with specific regulations on power, speed and use. Verify the current commenced regulations and any local rules before charging — this area changed recently.

Whether a device is a mechanically propelled vehicle affects insurance, tax, licence and drink-driving application. Check power output and the current statutory definition for the device in front of you.
No insurance/tax where the device qualifies as MPV; dangerous/careless use; footpath use; carrying passengers; underage use; modified/de-restricted units exceeding limits.

Treat as RTC: scene preservation, device seizure and examination (speed capability, modification), rider and driver accounts, CCTV. Establish the device's classification early — it drives the charges.

Seize and examine the device; photograph; note make/model and any de-restriction; establish speed capability; identify rider; medical evidence for injuries; map to the correct RTA provisions as currently in force.

items: Where lawfully on premises/at a scene and an item is evidence of an offence, it may be seized to preserve it. Record power and grounds.
Seize and retain evidence found during a search under warrant or lawful power.
Seize controlled drugs and related items on reasonable suspicion.
Seizure/detention of vehicles (no insurance s.41 RTA 1994; and MPV seizure powers) — verify the specific provision.
Cash seizure under Proceeds of Crime / CJA (money-laundering) provisions — high threshold, specialist input.
Photograph in situ, exhibit ref, sealed, labelled, PEMS, handover log. A seizure without continuity is a challenge waiting to happen.
